SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Given the summary, the experimental evidence appears shallow: datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics are not specified, and there is no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance to support the claimed state-of-the-art. Ablation studies and sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of prototypes, loss terms, joint vs separate training) are not reported, which is critical for validating the multi-prototype design. Related work coverage is said to be adequate, and a limitation of prior work (training cost, vocab size) is discussed, but reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are missing. From a theory-focused methodological perspective these omissions prevent rigorous validation of the main claims. | B(2): Based solely on the provided summary, the empirical evidence is shallow: key experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics) and hyperparameters are not specified despite a SOTA claim, which prevents validating results. No explicit mention of ablation or sensitivity analyses, and reproducibility is weak (code/checkpoints not reported). Related work coverage is described as adequate, and prior-method limitations are noted, but failure modes and quantitative reproduceable detail are largely missing, so the paper lacks sufficient depth to fully support its claims.

reviewer_a.justification: Given the summary, the experimental evidence appears shallow: datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics are not specified, and there is no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance to support the claimed state-of-the-art. Ablation studies and sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of prototypes, loss terms, joint vs separate training) are not reported, which is critical for validating the multi-prototype design. Related work coverage is said to be adequate, and a limitation of prior work (training cost, vocab size) is discussed, but reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are missing. From a theory-focused methodological perspective these omissions prevent rigorous validation of the main claims.

reviewer_b.justification: Based solely on the provided summary, the empirical evidence is shallow: key experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics) and hyperparameters are not specified despite a SOTA claim, which prevents validating results. No explicit mention of ablation or sensitivity analyses, and reproducibility is weak (code/checkpoints not reported). Related work coverage is described as adequate, and prior-method limitations are noted, but failure modes and quantitative reproduceable detail are largely missing, so the paper lacks sufficient depth to fully support its claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=1] A(1): Based on the provided summary the paper does not report any baseline comparisons ("Baselines compared: none mentioned") nor specify evaluation metrics or datasets, so it fails to situate itself against prior methods. From a theory-focused methodological stance, this is unacceptable: without controlled, fair comparisons and acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably, claims of state-of-the-art are unsubstantiated. The manuscript therefore provides no meaningful empirical comparison to assess its contributions. | B(1): The summary reports no baseline comparisons ("Baselines compared: none mentioned") yet claims state-of-the-art results; this is a critical omission. From my applied-NLP perspective, the paper fails to present even basic, recent competing methods or fairness details (datasets, metrics, conditions) needed to judge improvements. Although related work coverage is said to be adequate, without empirical comparisons and acknowledgement of when prior methods perform similarly or better, the evaluation is not meaningful.

reviewer_a.justification: Based on the provided summary the paper does not report any baseline comparisons ("Baselines compared: none mentioned") nor specify evaluation metrics or datasets, so it fails to situate itself against prior methods. From a theory-focused methodological stance, this is unacceptable: without controlled, fair comparisons and acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably, claims of state-of-the-art are unsubstantiated. The manuscript therefore provides no meaningful empirical comparison to assess its contributions.

reviewer_b.justification: The summary reports no baseline comparisons ("Baselines compared: none mentioned") yet claims state-of-the-art results; this is a critical omission. From my applied-NLP perspective, the paper fails to present even basic, recent competing methods or fairness details (datasets, metrics, conditions) needed to judge improvements. Although related work coverage is said to be adequate, without empirical comparisons and acknowledgement of when prior methods perform similarly or better, the evaluation is not meaningful.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies the established idea of multi-prototype (multi-sense) embeddings to mention representation and couples this with joint modeling of textual contexts and KB entities; this is a reasonable and coherent extension but not a radical conceptual departure from prior multi-prototype and joint text-KB work. The introduction of an "efficient language-model-based" disambiguation is potentially useful but, from the summary, appears to be an engineering-level enhancement rather than a fundamentally new theoretical perspective. Given the absence of described novel theoretical guarantees, formal analysis, or clear contrasts to concrete prior methods in the summary, I assess the contribution as a moderate incremental advance that would inform practitioners but may not surprise experts deeply familiar with multi-prototype and joint embedding literature. | B(3): The paper adapts the well-known multi-prototype embedding idea to mention representations and combines textual context with KB entities, which is a plausible but incremental extension of existing multi-sense embeddings and joint text–knowledge approaches. The proposed efficient LM-based disambiguation and joint learning appear practically useful, but based on the provided summary these techniques read as non-trivial engineering refinements rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. Because the formulation addresses a well-studied problem (entity/mention ambiguity) and the summary does not claim or evidence a novel theoretical framework or radically different approach from prior joint text-and-knowledge work, the contribution feels moderately novel rather than highly original.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies the established idea of multi-prototype (multi-sense) embeddings to mention representation and couples this with joint modeling of textual contexts and KB entities; this is a reasonable and coherent extension but not a radical conceptual departure from prior multi-prototype and joint text-KB work. The introduction of an "efficient language-model-based" disambiguation is potentially useful but, from the summary, appears to be an engineering-level enhancement rather than a fundamentally new theoretical perspective. Given the absence of described novel theoretical guarantees, formal analysis, or clear contrasts to concrete prior methods in the summary, I assess the contribution as a moderate incremental advance that would inform practitioners but may not surprise experts deeply familiar with multi-prototype and joint embedding literature.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper adapts the well-known multi-prototype embedding idea to mention representations and combines textual context with KB entities, which is a plausible but incremental extension of existing multi-sense embeddings and joint text–knowledge approaches. The proposed efficient LM-based disambiguation and joint learning appear practically useful, but based on the provided summary these techniques read as non-trivial engineering refinements rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. Because the formulation addresses a well-studied problem (entity/mention ambiguity) and the summary does not claim or evidence a novel theoretical framework or radically different approach from prior joint text-and-knowledge work, the contribution feels moderately novel rather than highly original.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): Overall presentation appears adequate but not outstanding. The paper is reported to have reasonable organization and adequate related-work coverage, and its main contributions (MPME model and LM-based disambiguation) are identifiable, but several presentation gaps reduce clarity: datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, and hyperparameter details are not specified, and there is no mention of consistent notation or effective figures/tables. From a theory-focused standpoint, the manuscript would benefit from clearer formalism (precise notation and definitions) and more explicit experimental reporting (tables, ablations, and variance measures) to make claims and comparisons reproducible and interpretable. | B(3): The summary indicates the paper is adequately written and states its main contributions early (the MPME model and LM-based disambiguation). However, important presentation elements are missing or not described in the summary: datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and whether figures/tables or code are provided. Notation and visual aids are not documented either, which makes some experimental and methodological sections hard to assess from a clarity/presentation standpoint.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall presentation appears adequate but not outstanding. The paper is reported to have reasonable organization and adequate related-work coverage, and its main contributions (MPME model and LM-based disambiguation) are identifiable, but several presentation gaps reduce clarity: datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, and hyperparameter details are not specified, and there is no mention of consistent notation or effective figures/tables. From a theory-focused standpoint, the manuscript would benefit from clearer formalism (precise notation and definitions) and more explicit experimental reporting (tables, ablations, and variance measures) to make claims and comparisons reproducible and interpretable.

reviewer_b.justification: The summary indicates the paper is adequately written and states its main contributions early (the MPME model and LM-based disambiguation). However, important presentation elements are missing or not described in the summary: datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and whether figures/tables or code are provided. Notation and visual aids are not documented either, which makes some experimental and methodological sections hard to assess from a clarity/presentation standpoint.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the work addresses an important problem and proposes promising, practically useful ideas (multi-prototype mention embeddings and an efficient LM-based disambiguator) that could influence practice. However, both also note substantial omissions in experimental detail, baselines, and reproducibility that limit confidence and immediate adoption. Given the clear conceptual merit but limited reported empirical rigor, a moderate impact score is most appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—representing ambiguous entity mentions via multi-prototype embeddings and jointly modeling text and knowledge-base entities—which, in principle, could meaningfully improve entity linking and representation learning. The proposed joint MPME framework and an efficient LM-based disambiguation are promising and could influence work on sense-aware mention representations. However, the summary omits critical details (datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, statistical variation and ablation studies), and no evidence of controlled comparisons is reported; these omissions make it unlikely the results will immediately change community practice or be widely adopted without stronger empirical validation. Given the conceptual merit but limited reported rigor, I judge the potential impact as moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper targets an important, practical problem—mention ambiguity and scalable joint text+KB representations—and proposes a concrete Multi-Prototype Mention Embedding (MPME) approach plus an efficient LM-based disambiguator, which together reportedly achieve state-of-the-art on an entity linking benchmark. From an applied-systems perspective this is likely to change how practitioners build disambiguation components (multi-prototype embeddings and efficient LM disambiguation are directly useful) and could be widely adopted. However, the summary omits dataset/baseline/details and code release, which tempers claims about reproducibility and broader transformational impact; thus I view the contribution as strong but not fully transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the topic and proposed approach are well within the scope of top NLP/ML venues and relevant to the intended audience. However, they also concur the submission omits important experimental and reproducibility details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters/code release), which weakens its suitability; given the clear topical fit but notable reporting gaps, a slightly reduced but positive score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—joint text and knowledge representations for entity linking using multi-prototype mention embeddings—is squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope and targets the right audience. However, based on the summary the paper omits critical methodological/evaluation details expected at this venue (no datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, hyperparameter reporting, or variance/statistical analysis cited), which undermines the reproducibility and rigor required for a strong fit. With fuller experimental reporting and controlled ablations (e.g., number of prototypes, training regimes, variance across runs) it would be a much better fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the scope of top NLP/ML venues: joint text–KB representation and entity linking are core conference topics and the proposed Multi-Prototype Mention Embedding (MPME) + LM-based disambiguation is directly relevant to that audience. The formulation and claimed empirical gains (SOTA on an entity linking benchmark) are appropriate, but the provided summary omits key experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics) and does not mention code release or hyperparameter reporting, which are important for reproducibility and full acceptance at a top venue. Overall, the submission is a good fit but would benefit from clearer evaluation reporting and release commitments.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the proposed method is plausible but the paper lacks essential experimental and methodological details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters, ablations, statistical testing and code) needed to verify the empirical claims. Because the core approach is not evidently flawed but the evidence and reproducibility are substantially incomplete, I assign a middling score reflecting serious soundness concerns without declaring the idea unsalvageable.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's central technical claim—MPME learns multiple sense embeddings and achieves state-of-the-art entity linking—is not sufficiently supported in the provided summary: datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and statistical significance are all unspecified, preventing verification. There are no reported mathematical derivations or proofs justifying why the joint modeling design or the LM-based disambiguation should outperform simpler alternatives, nor are controlled ablations (e.g., number of prototypes, loss terms, joint vs. separate training) or complexity/latency analyses presented. Given these missing methodological and empirical controls, the work contains significant unsupported claims that weaken its technical soundness.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a plausible technical approach (multi-prototype mention embeddings + LM-based disambiguation) and claims SOTA on an entity linking benchmark, but the provided summary lacks critical experimental details: datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, and hyperparameters are not specified and code release is not mentioned. Without these elements I cannot verify that the main empirical claims are well-supported, and there is no information on mathematical derivations or statistical significance testing. Thus there are notable concerns about the completeness and reproducibility of the evidence, though the methodology itself appears reasonable rather than fundamentally flawed.

main_claims: Propose a novel Multi-Prototype Mention Embedding (MPME) model to handle ambiguity of entity mentions by learning multiple sense embeddings for each mention.
Jointly model words from textual contexts and entities from a knowledge base to learn the multiple sense (multi-prototype) mention embeddings.
Design an efficient language-model-based approach to disambiguate each mention to a specific sense.
Qualitative and quantitative analyses demonstrate the high quality of the word, entity and multi-prototype mention embeddings.
Applied the disambiguation method and multi-prototype mention embeddings to an entity linking benchmark dataset and achieved state-of-the-art.
_summary.methodology: Learn multiple sense embeddings per mention by jointly modeling textual context words and knowledge-base entities (Multi-Prototype Mention Embedding), and disambiguate mentions to specific senses using an efficient language-model-based approach.
limitations: Prior joint text-and-knowledge methods suffer from expensive training and have great limitations on the size of word and entity vocabularies (cited as issues for previous work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - joint text and knowledge representations / entity linking

reviewer_a.name: Theory-Focused Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning and computational linguistics with a track record of theoretical work on representation learning and Bayesian models of ambiguity.
traits: Demands formal or conceptual justification for model components and will deduct points if no theoretical motivation or proof is provided for a key claim (e.g., why multi-prototype embeddings capture ambiguity better than alternatives).
Penalises empirical papers that lack controlled ablations isolating each design choice; expects an explicit ablation for number of prototypes, loss terms, and joint vs. separate training.

Requires statistical significance reporting (e.g., confidence intervals or p-values) across multiple runs for main benchmarks and will downgrade papers that report single-run numbers without variance.

Skeptical of claimed efficiency gains without formal complexity analysis or wall-clock comparisons on realistic hardware; will lower scores if efficiency/latency claims are qualitative or only microbenchmarked.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on conceptual clarity and theoretical justification; I reward principled contributions and penalize papers that present engineering improvements without rigorous analysis.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Systems Expert
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist with extensive experience building and evaluating large-scale entity linking and knowledge-integrated language systems used in production and benchmarks.
traits: Rewards papers that achieve clear empirical improvements on standard benchmarks (SOTA) and will give higher scores when gains are consistent and meaningful on end tasks like entity linking.

Favors practical, efficient methods and will upweight contributions that demonstrate scalability, low-latency disambiguation, or compatibility with existing LM pipelines (expects throughput/latency numbers but is forgiving if not exhaustive).
Values thorough qualitative and quantitative analysis demonstrating real-world utility (e.g., error analyses, case studies, human-readable examples) and will score up if these are present.
Prefers reproducibility: awards extra credit if code, pre-trained embeddings, or model checkpoints are released; will penalize papers that make strong empirical claims but do not commit to release.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize empirical impact, scalability, and practical usefulness; I reward well-engineered solutions that move the state of the art and include analyses demonstrating real-world behavior.
